summary: Modal announcing their own product updates is inherently promotional but verifiable through actual product testing—no independent vetting, heavy conflict of interest, but legitimate company with track record.
career_angle: Product update announcements are useful for staying current on tools you might use, but always verify new features independently before betting critical infrastructure on them.

dimensions.name: Source Authority
dimensions.rationale: Modal is the definitive authority on their own product features and capabilities. However, they're also the seller, creating obvious authority-via-self-interest.

dimensions.name: Evidence Quality
dimensions.rationale: Product update blogs typically describe features without external benchmarks or data. Claims are demonstration-based rather than data-backed.

dimensions.name: Reproducibility
dimensions.rationale: Any user can sign up and test Modal Notebooks or sandbox features directly. Claims are fully reproducible by trying the product.

dimensions.name: Conflict of Interest
dimensions.rationale: Massive COI—Modal profits directly from making their product sound essential and updated. They have zero incentive to highlight limitations or failures.

dimensions.name: Consensus
dimensions.rationale: No expert consensus to verify against. This is internal product news, not a claim about the broader market or competing approaches.

dimensions.name: Specificity
dimensions.rationale: Title names specific features (Modal Notebooks, sandbox idle timeouts), which is good. Feature descriptions are likely concrete, though marketing language may oversell benefits.

dimensions.name: Track Record
dimensions.rationale: Modal is an established, venture-backed company with a multi-year track record of delivering serverless infrastructure. They generally ship what they announce.
